speaker: interviewer
text: It's probably like-- in certain periods, we used to be really close. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Saw him many, many times. He gained weight though.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gained weight. He was too heavy. Her, too. They love to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date, December 19th, 1994. Survivor, Adolf Deutsch. Interviewer, Carol Stulberg. City, Los Angeles. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Carol Stulberg, and I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Adolf Deutsch, Monday, December 19, 1994 in Los Angeles. Mr. Deutsch, please give us your name at birth and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Adolf Deutsch. In Yiddish, they call me [YIDDISH] And I was born August 8th, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city and what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Sighet Maramures. That's a part of Romania. It's in Transylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Sighet for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-I-G-H-E-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob Deutsch. My mother's name was Mindel Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Mindel Basch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of your brothers and sisters, and the approximate years of their birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My older sister's name was Yehudit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- oh, my God, it's so hard to go back through the years. She would have been-- she would have been today about 82 years. And she was the oldest. And after this, I have a brother by the name of Moshe. He was born two years after my sister. And then I have another brother by the name of Meyer Isaac Deutsch. After Myer Isaac, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I have another brother by the name of Leizer Dov Deutsch. Deutsch. Then I have a sister by the name of Idele, same name as, by the way, as my wife's name. They were first cousins. Then I have-- had a brother in Montreal by the name of Simcha Deutsch. I still have a sister, she lives in Brooklyn now. Her name is Hindi Deutsch. And then I come after her. And after me, I still have a sister who's alive. And her name is Risi. And after Risi, there  was another brother by the name of Sruli. And there was a youngest one by the name of Perela, Perry. Those were the 11 children, brothers and sisters, that my father and mother, Jacob and Mindel, had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do to support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] He was a scholar. He-- he was studying. And he also was a merchant. They had a-- a store. Those days, in Europe, they used to buy clothing through material that was rolled up in rolls. I'm trying to think of the name what they call it here. It like-- they call, silk, yarns, in the rolls--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like dry goods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like dry goods, yeah. It was a dry goods store, and my mother also used to help out. So he had a little more time to go to study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your life to us before the war. Your childhood, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] There was a time that I remember as a kid that we were very poor. There was, I think-- it was, I think, in 1938 or '39, there was a crash in Romania. The money had no value. And I remember that we were so poor, there was no food in the house. People had no jobs. Nobody had money. The only way we survived, we used to go to my grandparents' house. We used to try and find food in their house. I remember climbing the trees to try and get from the-- the highest branches, there was still left some apples [LAUGHS] to try-- to try and eat to survive. Really, there was no food. At that time, it was such a hunger. I remember that they brought in ground soya. And they used to make-- they used to cook it up, bake it up, make it like bread. People didn't know what soya will do for you. And the kids used to eat that bread, and it used to blow them up. When I saw the pictures-- the last time, one they had-- remember, we had, in one of the African countries, that the kids were blowing up. They were skeletons, but they had big bellies. That's how the kids looked in my times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I realized what these children, unfortunately, went through in Africa. To remember how the kids used to die-- everybody's house had someone that died. There was such a hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, as a child, we used to think about it. When a person goes through life, good days, bad days, there were times we had plenty of food in the house, and there were times we had no food in the house. We used to go to sleep-- many a times. We used to cry ourselves to sleep. That's how hungry we used to be. There was no way we can get any help. They were tough times. Then, as I grew up already a little bit, I tried to go to people's houses. I used to have a couple hand clippers, used to go and cut [? all ?] people's hair to be able to earn a few pennies to be able to buy a pretzel or a slice of bread or a cookie or a candy, because we had no money in the house. As a child, I remember, I was, I think, eight and a half or nine years old, and I did this to be able to earn some money for myself. And I used to go from house to house and ask who needs to have their hair cut. That's how-- that was my first adventure to become [LAUGHS] a businessman. I couldn't believe myself, what a person has to go through in life to get themselves started, but that was my beginning. And I think because of the hardships that I had while I was a kid, it helped me also to survive. Because those boys that came from the better homes, they were used to good food, and good homes, and clean homes. They couldn't make it in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was-- when do you remember the first sign of trouble? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sign of trouble I remember when I was away from home. I was sent away to yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. And there came an order. The only way the orders came those days was announced through a-- a drum. There used to come a drummer. He used to go from street to street and drum that he has news to say. And everybody gather around this man, and he was telling everybody whatever news there was to be told.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a time that he said that the school is going to be closed. So we already knew that something is brewing, some-- some kind of trouble is-- is-- is on its way. And that's when they evacuated the school, and we went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my way home, the train wasn't going all the way through to my town. So it stopped off at my uncle's house, by the name of-- the name-- the city's names, the little small city, was named Krolla Nadkara. And I slept over there over the night. And the following morning, there was another train going a little further. You know, trains didn't go like over here, from one end to the other. We had few hours of train here, a few hours of train there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And then I went home, and I stayed in the house. It wasn't that I could go to another school, because the schools were closed already. So that's when trouble started. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we tried to help out my grandfather's store. My mother didn't have a store then. What we did, we-- we got a concession of the bathhouse from the city. You know, those days, people didn't have water connections in the house or bathrooms in the house. Everybody had to go to a central bathhouse. And that's where I started to help out. And-- and this is how it continued till the end, till we were gathered to go to the ghetto. Until then, we had freedom to go around the streets, but we did have to wear a star to let everybody know who are, so we should be recognized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience a lot of anti-semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It only-- while-- while the-- we were under the Romanian occupation, it wasn't so bad. But then we became occupied in 1940 from the Hungarians, that's when we realized that they're not the same-- the situation wasn't the same. While they were trying to-- trying to call the Jews by their names, and my father used to say, those are not the Hungarian [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, the Hungarians, they have whiskers, so their-- their expression that they used to use that the Hungarians are the whiskers, the [NON-ENGLISH]. These are not the same [NON-ENGLISH] that I knew. Because my father was really born in a small town, it was only Hungarians. That's all there was is Hungarian-- Hungarian Gentiles and Jews. And they lived peacefully.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Hungarians came in and start screaming dirty Jew, that's where my father realized this is not right. But [SIGHS] normal life continued. People had stores. People went to work, whoever had work. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then when the Germans came in, that's when the real trouble started. And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 19-- in 1943, they came into our city. I remember vividly. It was a Thursday. It was March-- March the 15th. And we didn't-- I mean, we didn't know what was really going on in the world, because we had no radios in the house. There was no television, like we have here today that you know everything is going on in the world. We were really living, like you say, in the back-- back woods, in the woods. But little by little, we had what we call now the underground radios. People did have radios where they [BACKGROUND NOISE] used to come into the shul every morning, where we used to come to pray. They used to come and tell us the news, that the Germans are here, and the Germans are doing this, and they're talking about the ghetto. We didn't know what the word ghetto means. That the people are going to have to leave their homes, that people left other people. We said, how could they. It couldn't be possible. Because I remember my mother saying that we've been there for a couple hundred years, you know, from generations, that why would anybody come and do anything harm for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mother used to talk about, oh, we have papers to show. Didn't mean nothing. We didn't know the reason why they're doing all this trouble because she was Jewish. And because she was Jewish, this is what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I remember, we-- we were-- people were gathered to go to the ghetto. But there's being a boy ready by the age of 18, 17 and a 1/2 to 18, they took all those boys, and they took us away from the city. And we marched to a place called Pollin-- Pollin Kabaletsk, small town. There was a-- a town. It was surrounded with big mountains. And on those mountains, they used to have-- they used to also have flat areas, they used to grow hay. And the military needed, those days, hay for the horses, because all the Germans were pulling their-- their-- their cannons, and all the wagons was pulled by-- not even horses, they're called-- they're a mixture of a mule and a horse. They're called mulesel. It's a horse that's stronger than a horse and stronger than a mule. And they-- it could even eat wood. So the only way to-- for them to be able to do their jobs, we had to go up in the hills and uncover the snow and try to take the hay in bundles and bring it down to a place, to a area, where there was a train waiting. When the train was full with hay, they used to take it away. Next day, there came another empty train. And we were there, about 250 boys. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we were doing this from morning to night. So while the people were in the ghetto, we were up there doing this kind of work. We didn't know what's going on at home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it happened to be pace of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already in that place, and my mother sent with one of employees that helped work in the mikveh, he sent some money and send me some potato latkes. Can you imagine receiving potato latkes in that place? But he had to smuggle in. It wasn't so easy for him to come, to look me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he gave me some pengos, he brought me some Hungarian money, and he brought me something to eat. That's all-- and I asked the man what is going on at home, he said he didn't want to tell me. He was afraid to tell us because we might rebel. We only found out what the ghetto was all about is when they brought us back three days before they were liberating taking away the camp. They were removing the people to put them on trains. That was the time that they brought all of us back, and we were also put on that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you were brought back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were already in the-- in the ghetto. And the following day, they came, and they told everybody to pack up whatever they had, and we were put into those big synagogues. We had those large synagogues. Each synagogues could hold like 400 or 500 people. Into each synagogue was put people. They were evacuated from their homes. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we realized that something is going on. We didn't know what-- what's going to happen to us, where they're going to take us, what-- where are we going to go. And we were gathered in the synagogues. We were there for another day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family together, yeah. The whole family, except some of my brothers and sisters, they were not home. They were already married. Some of them were away. Some of them were in the military. But with my youngest brothers and sisters, we were all there. And then they told us to march to go to a train. We marched to the train. They were-- actually, they were-- they were divided into three different transports. Every day, there was a transport of a train had to be filled up. When the train was filled, they told the rest of the people to go back. And then the following day, they took those people. We were going on the train, I remember it was between five and six days. It was-- because we arrived-- the last day, we arrived at night. We arrived to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] When we arrived in Auschwitz, we see the whole camp was lit up, just like this light here, from every side. It was big lights. And dogs barking, and soldiers, the SS were-- surrounded. All of us, as we arrived in Auschwitz. But on the way coming to Auschwitz, we didn't have any food, only what we took along with us. The last couple days, already, we were eating dry wheat, barley. We were chewing on barley. We were eating dry beans because we were so hungry. There was no-- nothing to eat. They didn't give us anything for all the duration, from the time that they took us away from-- from our city. Some people couldn't make it. Some people died. We had a lot of people dead when we arrived to Auschwitz. And [SIGHS] the stink on the train, was-- you cannot describe it. It's unbelievable how people-- we were packed like herrings, so you can imagine what it felt like. We couldn't sleep. There was no way to sleep, so we slept standing up, leaning one on another. When we arrived, already, the train was like half empty because some of them couldn't make it. When we arrived to Auschwitz, I remember they were-- [SIGHS] they were hollering in German, but we understood because we spoke Jewish in the house. So we understood a little bit what they were talking about. Then they took the younger ones on one side, and the older ones on another side, mothers and kids on another side. And we stayed the whole night to be-- to wait, to tell us what to do. But, meanwhile, we saw that the older people that they were already in the camp, they used to come around with those wagons, no horses. It was human-- human horses. Six people used to pull a wagon. And those people that couldn't walk, the elderly or disabled, they used to put them on those wagons, and they used take them away. We didn't know what's going on, what's happening, where they take them. But then, after a while, we found out that they went into the crematoriums. But we didn't know, the first couple days, we didn't know, really, what was going on, because we were selected to go to labor. So I waited there a whole night. That day that we arrived, they told us to undress and take a shower. And after the shower, they gave us suits that looked like pajamas, pajamas that I have today, that's the pajamas. That was the suit that we had. Then after this was done, we-- we were told to stay in the line. They were giving us numbers. Everybody was-- whoever was going to that particular camp to that time, people used to get numbers. I have a number on my hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your number is?

speaker: interviewee
text: 83146. I'm not sure, I have to look.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you see what it say? Is it that--

speaker: interviewee
text: 46? Is that a 4?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard to tell from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you see through the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, you would say, for sure, three months later, you're going to have AIDS, because they used the long needles. And every stop that you see, every spot that you see, was a long needle going down, all the way down to the bones. And the next few days, my arm was so swollen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't even know what was going to happen. People didn't have different needles for everybody else. The same needle was used one after another. You think back what a person went through, for today's time, you'd never be able to survive. But, somehow, I survived. And we were there for three days. We were-- from Auschwitz, we went to Birkenau. In Birkenau, they put together a transport of workers to go to a working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you separated from your family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was separated from [SIGHS] everyone else. My father and mother, I didn't see after that no more. And I didn't know about my sisters. Three days before I was transported away, I met my younger brother. And in those days, in the camps, if you had a cigarette, you could buy a piece of bread, can buy a piece of margarine, can buy a piece of Worsht. And so what I did, I had a brand new pair of shoes that I came in with. I took my shoes, and I sold them for a box of cigarettes. And I gave the box of cigarettes to my younger brother. He should be able to survive. I figured I'll be able to survive somehow. But for him to survive, if he's hot to have something to sell, then he can get himself an extra slice of bread. But after that, I didn't see him no more. What happened to him after that, I don't know. What we found out afterwards that the following few days, they took all the youngsters, that they took them into the crematoriums. Because I just met a friend of mine that we were in together, and he says he stayed on there for about six weeks in Auschwitz. He said the [INAUDIBLE], the-- the-- the cries and the screams, and the-- it was-- you could hear it miles. So this is what probably happened to him too. He went to-- to the crematorium. And my youngest sister was with my older three sisters together for a little while in Auschwitz. And then they selected the youngsters, so they took her away. My other three sisters were sent to labor camps, different camps, different places. This is how they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent to a camp about 10 kilometers from Birkenau. It was called Buna. That was a camp, about 60,000 people, tremendous camp. And we used to go to work every day. They used to put us together, five in a line. And we used to go every morning, used to wake us up early in the morning, it was still dark, because he had to stay in the line for a couple hours because we had to be counted. Every time we came in from work, before you went to work, always had to stay, what it's called, appel. The word appel, I don't know if you've heard of it, is you have to stay and to be counted. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- we stayed in that camp for about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go to work every morning. I worked as a schlosser. It's called a locksmith. Locksmith, you know, is not only locks. You could do everything that has to do with steel, with iron. We used to make pipes because we developed-- we also worked in that place where we established a new factory, so you had to set up the place. We used to heat up pipes to be able to connect those machines. And I've worked with Germans, and I've worked with Poles, and I've worked with people from all over the world. They were in civil clothes, but they came to work every day in the factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were in this camp, it was clean. We were 200 in a barrack. And we got, every morning, something to eat before we went to work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was very cold. It was winter time. And to survive-- the only way we survive, we slept five together in one cot to keep each warm. It's funny, we were talking about it when I was in-- in-- in-- in Washington. I looked at the cots. I said to my daughter, you see, I slept first in the middle, then I slept on the outside, then I slept the second one, because kept on changing each other so you should keep each the warm in the winter nights. It's just the unbelievable where a person-- how a person can survive. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] After about six months, they start to bomb the camp because there was a lot of factories. They were trying to get to the factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who was bombing the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, we used to see the Russians. Because we could see the stars with the Russian writing. They came so low that we could see the Russian planes. At night, used to come either the English or the Americans, because they used to lit up to the skies. It was so bright during the night when they came to bomb, they used to lit up the whole sky. So you could see the Americans, the English. And when they start to bomb, we obviously used to run out of the barracks, used to lay in the fields. And we waited till the siren was called that it's over, then we went back to the-- to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was going only for a couple of weeks because then they evacuated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they start to evacuate us, that's when the real trouble started. People were freezing to death. Like I told you before, we all had only pajamas on. It was 20, 30 below zero, so you can imagine how cold it was. Today, you would probably put on underwear and a-- and a jacket, and undershirts, and a winter coat, and we only had the pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people just froze to death. As we marched, there was always less and less. We marched, the whole camp marched away. We evacuated the whole camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the time we arrived somewhere, it was like one third was less. And if someone sat down, they said they don't want to walk no more, they just give him a bullet, that's all.
